findings.summary: Governance cycles run on quarters; AI deploys in weeks. Decisions are made on stale information.
Task-specific models outperform general-purpose ones at lower cost. Design for interchange, not lock-in.
Multi-agent systems are the emerging pattern — but bring cascade failures and feedback loops without precedent.
Automating a broken process produces an automated broken process. Redesign for agent strengths first.
70% of AI value lies in revenue processes, but most portfolios target productivity. The metrics don't match.
AI agents need onboarding, performance management, and retirement. They are workforce participants, not tools.
Only 22% of organisations have AI-ready infrastructure. The brain was built without the nervous system.
AI agents operate on explicit knowledge. Most organisations have never codified how they actually work.
Sovereignty is a first-order design input. Organisations treating it as post-hoc compliance will build in the wrong place.

cells.speed: Maturity scores are quarterly snapshots. Agent risk profiles change weekly. By the time the board sees the assessment, the state it describes no longer exists.
cells.predictability: Pass/fail compliance cannot assess a system that is 99.5% compliant. Assessment must become statistical — distributions and confidence intervals, not checkboxes.
cells.scale: Scope expands from dozens of applications to hundreds of models, agents, and data pipelines, each versioned independently. Manual inventory is no longer viable.
cells.readability: You cannot assess what has not been written down. Most organisations have never codified how they actually work — and agents need that codification to operate.
cells.observability: Risk registers have no entry for 'agent produces confidently wrong output that passes every health check.' Assessments report green while agent risks are entirely unaddressed.

cells.speed: Roadmaps compress from years to sprints. Target architectures must be event-driven specifications that adapt, not fixed blueprints awaiting milestone review.
cells.predictability: Architecture defines guardrails, blast-radius boundaries, and escalation paths — not step-by-step prescriptions that assume deterministic execution.
cells.scale: Artifacts multiply faster than architects can review. Without auto-generation and automated validation, planning becomes the bottleneck that blocks delivery.
cells.readability: Target architectures must be machine-readable specifications agents can consume, not slide decks only humans read. Every capability needs a delivery model: human, augmented, or agent-native.
cells.observability: Every deployment plan must include semantic monitoring and kill switches. The failure mode is an agent that returns correct-looking results while causing business harm — undetectable by traditional means.

cells.speed: If governance takes longer than deployment, it governs the past. Policy-as-code running in deployment pipelines is the only option that matches agent deployment cadence.
cells.predictability: Non-deterministic outputs break audit trails. Compliance must shift from point-in-time review to continuous probabilistic monitoring with statistical thresholds.
cells.scale: Governance workload scales linearly with agents but governance capacity does not. Automate routine compliance or the EA function becomes the bottleneck to every AI initiative.
cells.readability: Governance rules that exist only as PDFs are unenforceable at agent speed. Every policy needs dual expression: human-readable for review, machine-executable for enforcement.
cells.observability: When an agent makes a confident wrong decision, the accountability chain — model provider to operator to business owner — must already be defined. You cannot trace what you did not instrument.

domain_summaries.label: EA Development
bullets: Development cycles compress from <mark>quarters to weeks</mark> — human review capacity becomes the binding constraint
Every capability needs a delivery model classification: human, augmented, or agent-native
Requirements management becomes event-driven and agent-fed, not a periodic human intake process
Architecture Vision must declare the agent strategy before downstream phases begin

domain_summaries.label: Governance
bullets: Governance speed must <mark>match deployment speed</mark> or agents deploy ungoverned
Architecture Board shifts to exception-based governance — automated pre-checks clear conformant deployments
Every policy needs dual expression: human-readable for review, machine-executable for enforcement
Compliance becomes continuous and probabilistic — agents drift between checkpoint reviews

domain_summaries.label: Repository
bullets: Repository must add <mark>Agent Registry and Model Register</mark> as mandatory components
The metamodel needs Agent, Model, and Guardrail as first-class entities
Repository exposes APIs for programmatic query by agents and compliance automation
Landscape refresh becomes volatility-driven: continuous for AI, deliberate for stable systems

domain_summaries.label: Roles & Skills
bullets: EA function becomes a <mark>human-agent hybrid team</mark> with its own agents as reference implementation
Every architect role carries agent governance accountability — domain architects become more T-shaped, not obsolete
New roles required: AI/Agent Architect, Agent Governance Lead, Agent Operations Architect
The single “AI and Expert Systems” skill line must expand to eight agent-specific competencies

domain_summaries.label: Risk & Security
bullets: Traditional risk matrices <mark>cannot score non-deterministic AI failures</mark> — new categories needed for cascade failure, hallucination, emergent behaviour
AI introduces attack vectors invisible to traditional security: prompt injection, model extraction, data poisoning
AI systems drift out of compliance without any code change — continuous monitoring replaces milestone reviews
Zero Trust applied to agents: per-session auth, minimum privilege, time-limited tokens
